content.text: Continued fractions provide a complete solution to Pell's equation.

content.title: Pell's Equation
content.content:
**Pell's equation** is $x^2 - dy^2 = 1$ where $d$ is a positive 
integer that is not a perfect square.

content.text:
The name is historical; Pell had little to do with it. The equation 
was studied by Brahmagupta, Bhaskara II, and Fermat, and solved 
completely by Lagrange.

content.title: Fundamental Solution
content.content:
Let $\sqrt{d} = [a_0; \overline{a_1, \ldots, a_r}]$ have period $r$.

If $r$ is even: $(x_1, y_1) = (p_{r-1}, q_{r-1})$ is the fundamental 
solution (smallest with $x, y > 0$).

If $r$ is odd: $(x_1, y_1) = (p_{2r-1}, q_{2r-1})$ is fundamental.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Solving $x^2 - 7y^2 = 1$
content.problem:
$\sqrt{7} = [2; \overline{1, 1, 1, 4}]$ has period 4 (even).

Computing convergents:
$$
p_0 &= 2, & q_0 &= 1 \\
p_1 &= 3, & q_1 &= 1 \\
p_2 &= 5, & q_2 &= 2 \\
p_3 &= 8, & q_3 &= 3
$$

Fundamental solution: $(8, 3)$. Check: $64 - 7 \cdot 9 = 64 - 63 = 1$ $\checkmark$

content.title: General Solution
content.content:
If $(x_1, y_1)$ is the fundamental solution, then all positive solutions 
are $(x_n, y_n)$ where
\[
x_n + y_n\sqrt{d} = (x_1 + y_1\sqrt{d})^n.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The negative Pell equation $x^2 - dy^2 = -1$ has solutions if and only 
if the period of $\sqrt{d}$'s continued fraction is odd.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
For $d = 2$: period is 1 (odd), so $x^2 - 2y^2 = -1$ has solutions. 
Indeed, $(1, 1)$: $1 - 2 = -1$.
